title: How are hash collisions handled in a HashMap?

answers.junior: HashMap handles hash collisions using a technique called separate chaining, where each bucket in the internal array holds a linked list of entries that hash to the same index. When two keys produce the same hash code (or map to the same bucket index after applying (n-1) & hash), the new entry is appended to the chain at that bucket.
answers.middle: HashMap handles hash collisions using a technique called separate chaining, where each bucket in the internal array holds a linked list of entries that hash to the same index. When two keys produce the same hash code (or map to the same bucket index after applying (n-1) & hash), the new entry is appended to the chain at that bucket. Prior to Java 8, this chain was always a plain linked list, giving O(n) worst-case lookup when many keys collide. Starting in Java 8, HashMap introduced a critical optimization: when the number of entries in a single bucket exceeds a threshold (TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8) and the overall table capacity is at least MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY (64), the linked list is converted into a balanced red-black tree, reducing worst-case lookup from O(n) to O(log n). Conversely, when entries are removed and the bucket size falls below UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD (6), the tree is converted back to a linked list.
answers.senior: HashMap handles hash collisions using a technique called separate chaining, where each bucket in the internal array holds a linked list of entries that hash to the same index. When two keys produce the same hash code (or map to the same bucket index after applying (n-1) & hash), the new entry is appended to the chain at that bucket. Prior to Java 8, this chain was always a plain linked list, giving O(n) worst-case lookup when many keys collide. Starting in Java 8, HashMap introduced a critical optimization: when the number of entries in a single bucket exceeds a threshold (TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8) and the overall table capacity is at least MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY (64), the linked list is converted into a balanced red-black tree, reducing worst-case lookup from O(n) to O(log n). Conversely, when entries are removed and the bucket size falls below UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD (6), the tree is converted back to a linked list. Collision resolution starts at the hash computation itself — HashMap applies a secondary hash function (h ^ (h >>> 16)) called "perturbation" to spread the higher bits of the hash code into the lower bits, reducing clustering and the likelihood of collisions. The actual bucket index is determined by (n - 1) & hash, which requires n to be a power of two (HashMap always rounds capacity up to the nearest power of two) for this bitwise AND to distribute evenly. Two keys that are equal by equals() will always occupy the same bucket since equal objects must have the same hashCode(); an inconsistent equals/hashCode contract is one of the most dangerous mistakes in custom key classes, causing entries to be permanently "lost." For production use, keys should have high-entropy, well-distributed hash codes — poor implementations (e.g., all keys returning 0) degrade HashMap to O(n) performance regardless of Java version. In security-sensitive contexts (like HTTP query parameter parsing), Java uses hash randomization (jdk.map.althashing.threshold) to prevent hash-flooding denial-of-service attacks where adversaries craft inputs to force maximum collisions.
